covered_sections: 2.2 Ionic Bonds>2.2.2 Formation of Ionic Bonds
2.2 Ionic Bonds>Factors Affecting the Formation of Ionic Bonds

mini_headings_covered: Lattice Energies from the Born-Haber Cycle

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: What is the octet rule in chemical bonding?

options.A: Atoms tend to gain, lose, or share electrons to achieve eight valence electrons.
options.B: Atoms always have exactly eight electrons in their outermost shell.
options.C: Only noble gases can have eight electrons in their valence shell.
options.D: Atoms transfer electrons to form exactly eight bonds with other atoms.

questions.hint: Consider the stable electron configuration of noble gases (except helium).
questions.explanation: The octet rule states that atoms tend to gain, lose, or share electrons to achieve an outer shell with eight electrons, similar to noble gases. Option B is incorrect because atoms do not always have eight electrons; ions and some molecules have exceptions.

source_trace.content_anchor: Atoms tend to gain or lose electrons until they have achieved an outer shell that contains an octet of electrons

explanations_tiered.definition: The octet rule is a chemical principle stating that atoms tend to combine in ways that give them eight valence electrons, achieving a stable noble gas electron configuration.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The octet rule explains why most atoms form bonds: to fill their outermost energy level with eight electrons. This is based on the stability of noble gases.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of atoms like people trying to collect a full set of eight stickers. They will trade or share stickers until each has a full set of eight.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The octet rule guides atom interactions: atoms gain, lose, or share electrons to achieve a full outer shell of eight electrons, matching the noble gas configuration.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The octet rule arises from quantum mechanical stability: an \(ns^2 np^6\) configuration has a filled valence shell, maximizing ionization energy and minimizing electron affinity. This drives bond formation.
reasoning_steps: Recall the definition of the octet rule from chemical bonding principles.
Identify the key action: gaining, losing, or sharing electrons.
Recognize the target: eight valence electrons (an octet).
Match to the option that includes all three actions.
misconceptions: The octet rule applies to all atoms without exception is incorrect because hydrogen and helium are stable with 2 electrons, and some elements can have expanded octets.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Octet rule: atoms react to gain 8 valence electrons (except H, He: 2).

glossary_entry.definition: Chemical rule that atoms tend to combine to have eight electrons in their valence shell.

flashcard.back: Atoms gain, lose, or share electrons to achieve eight valence electrons, like noble gases.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What drives atoms to form chemical bonds according to the octet rule?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: The drive to achieve a stable outer shell of eight valence electrons, like noble gases.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Tendency of atoms to gain, lose, or share electrons to obtain eight valence electrons.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Octet rule: 8 e⁻ in valence shell = stable.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Common misconception: students think all atoms need 8. Emphasize H and He exceptions.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the octet rule in bonding
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. The octet rule defines the tendency to achieve eight valence electrons through gain, loss, or sharing.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: A compound forms between a metal from Group 1 and a nonmetal from Group 17. Which type of chemical bond is most likely present?
questions.hint: Consider the electronegativity difference and electron transfer tendency.
questions.explanation: Metals from Group 1 have low ionization energy and lose electrons easily, while nonmetals from Group 17 have high electron affinity. This electron transfer forms an ionic bond. Covalent bonds typically occur between two nonmetals.

explanations_tiered.definition: An ionic bond is formed by the electrostatic attraction between positively and negatively charged ions resulting from electron transfer.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Ionic bonds occur between metals and nonmetals due to large electronegativity differences, leading to electron transfer and ion formation.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine a metal atom gives away an electron to a nonmetal atom. Now they have opposite charges and stick together like magnets.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Group 1 metals have one valence electron and low ionization energy. Group 17 nonmetals need one electron. Electron transfer creates \(Na^+\) and \(Cl^-\), which attract ionically.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The large difference in electronegativity (e.g., Na = 0.93, Cl = 3.16) favors complete electron transfer rather than sharing. The resulting lattice energy stabilizes the solid.
reasoning_steps: Identify the groups: Group 1 (metal) and Group 17 (nonmetal).
Recall that metal + nonmetal typically forms ionic bonds.
Eliminate metallic (metal+metal) and covalent (nonmetal+nonmetal).
misconceptions: All bonds between different elements are ionic. This is false; many are polar covalent.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Ionic bond: metal + nonmetal → electron transfer → ion attraction.

glossary_entry.definition: Electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions formed by electron transfer.

flashcard.front: What type of bond forms between a Group 1 metal and a Group 17 nonmetal?
flashcard.back: Ionic bond, due to electron transfer from metal to nonmetal.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why do Group 1 metals and Group 17 nonmetals form ionic bonds?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: The metal loses an electron to become a cation, the nonmetal gains it to become an anion, and they attract electrostatically.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Bond from electrostatic attraction of cations and anions after electron transfer.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce that bond type is a spectrum; ionic is one extreme.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Identify bond types from element positions
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Metals donate electrons, nonmetals accept them, creating ions that attract ionically.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which Lewis electron-dot symbol correctly represents the valence electrons for a sulfur atom (\(\mathrm{S}\))?
questions.hint: Sulfur is in Group 16 and has six valence electrons.
questions.explanation: Sulfur has the electron configuration \([\mathrm{Ne}]3s^2 3p^4\), giving six valence electrons. Lewis symbols place dots around the element symbol, with one dot per valence electron, paired when possible. Option C shows six dots correctly.

source_trace.content_anchor: dots placed around the symbol to represent the valence electrons

explanations_tiered.definition: A Lewis electron-dot symbol represents the valence electrons of an atom as dots placed around the chemical symbol.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The number of dots equals the number of valence electrons. Dots are placed singly on each side before pairing.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Draw dots around the element like points on a compass (top, bottom, left, right). Add one dot to each side before pairing them up.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Sulfur has 6 valence electrons (Group 16). Lewis symbol: two paired dots on two sides and two single dots on the remaining sides, totaling 6 dots.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Lewis symbols follow the aufbau principle for representation: each of the four sides gets one electron before pairing. For sulfur \(3s^2 3p^4\), the \(3s^2\) are paired, and \(3p^4\) gives one pair and two singles.
reasoning_steps: Determine the group number of sulfur (Group 16).
Recall that Group 16 elements have 6 valence electrons.
Draw a Lewis symbol with 6 dots, following the pairing rule (max 2 per side).
Match to the option with exactly 6 dots.
misconceptions: Students often put all dots on one side or forget that paired dots represent two electrons.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Lewis dot: dots = valence e⁻, one per side before pairing.

glossary_entry.definition: Representation of an atom showing its valence electrons as dots around the chemical symbol.

flashcard.front: How many dots are in the Lewis symbol for sulfur?
flashcard.back: Six dots, because sulfur has 6 valence electrons.

explanations_tiered.revision_note: Sulfur Lewis dot: 6 dots (two pairs, two singles).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the Lewis dot symbol for sulfur?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Chemical symbol with dots for valence electrons.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Practice drawing Lewis symbols for Groups 1, 2, 13-18.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Draw Lewis symbols for main group elements
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Six valence electrons = 6 dots arranged with pairing.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Why does the formation of \(\mathrm{LiF(s)}\) from \(\mathrm{Li(g)}\) and \(\mathrm{F(g)}\) release a large amount of energy overall, despite the electron transfer step being endothermic?

options.A: The ionization energy of Li is zero.
options.B: The electron affinity of F is positive and large.
options.C: The lattice energy released when ions form the solid overcomes the endothermic steps.

questions.hint: Consider the energy changes when gaseous ions come together to form a solid crystal.
questions.explanation: Although forming \(\mathrm{Li^+}\) and \(\mathrm{F^-}\) from atoms requires energy (IE + EA), the lattice energy released when these ions arrange into a crystal is very large (exothermic). This lattice energy outweighs the earlier energy input, making overall formation exothermic.

source_trace.content_anchor: enormous release of energy when the ions come together and form a solid

explanations_tiered.definition: Lattice energy is the enthalpy change when one mole of an ionic solid separates into its gaseous ions.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The Born-Haber cycle shows that the highly exothermic lattice energy compensates for endothermic steps like atomization, ionization, and bond dissociation.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Making ions costs energy, but when those ions snap together into a crystal, they release even more energy — like a battery charging then discharging a bigger jolt.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The electron transfer (IE + EA) is endothermic, but the lattice energy (\(U\)) for \(\mathrm{LiF}\) is \(-1050 \mathrm{kJ/mol}\). The sum of all steps is negative, making \(\mathrm{LiF}\) formation spontaneous.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Hess's law applied via the Born-Haber cycle: \(\Delta H_f^\circ = \Delta H_{\text{atom}}(\mathrm{Li}) + \frac{1}{2}D_{\mathrm{Cl}_2} + \mathrm{IE}_1 + \mathrm{EA} + U\). Solving for \(U\) yields a large negative value that drives the reaction.
reasoning_steps: Identify the endothermic steps: atomization, bond dissociation, ionization.
Identify the exothermic steps: electron affinity (small) and lattice energy (large).
Recognize that the large negative lattice energy is the dominant term.
Conclude that lattice energy overcomes earlier endothermic steps.
misconceptions: Ionic bond formation is always exothermic in every step. False; ionization is endothermic.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Lattice energy > endothermic steps → stable ionic solid.

glossary_entry.definition: Energy released when gaseous ions form one mole of ionic solid.

flashcard.front: Why is LiF formation exothermic overall if making ions costs energy?
flashcard.back: The lattice energy released when ions form the crystal is much larger than the energy required to create the ions.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What role does lattice energy play in ionic compound formation?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: It provides the large exothermic release that makes overall formation spontaneous.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Enthalpy change when gaseous ions form an ionic solid.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Lattice energy = huge exothermic, drives ionic bond formation.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use Born-Haber cycle to show how lattice energy compensates.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain energy changes in ionic bond formation
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Lattice energy is the large exothermic step that overcomes endothermic IE and atomization.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.structural_anchor: Lattice Energies from the Born-Haber Cycle
questions.question: Given the data below for \(\mathrm{NaCl}\), calculate the lattice energy (\(U\)) in \(\mathrm{kJ/mol}\). \(\Delta H_f^\circ(\mathrm{NaCl}) = -411\ \mathrm{kJ/mol}\), sublimation of Na: \(+108\ \mathrm{kJ/mol}\), bond dissociation of \(\mathrm{Cl_2}\): \(+240\ \mathrm{kJ/mol}\), first ionization energy of Na: \(+496\ \mathrm{kJ/mol}\), electron affinity of Cl: \(-349\ \mathrm{kJ/mol}\).
questions.hint: Use Hess's law: \(\Delta H_f = \Delta H_{\text{sub}} + \frac{1}{2}D + \mathrm{IE} + \mathrm{EA} + U\). Solve for \(U\).
questions.explanation: The Born-Haber cycle gives \(U = \Delta H_f - (\Delta H_{\text{sub}} + \frac{1}{2}D + \mathrm{IE} + \mathrm{EA})\). Substituting: \(U = -411 - [108 + 120 + 496 + (-349)] = -411 - (375) = -786\ \mathrm{kJ/mol}\). The negative sign indicates energy released.

source_trace.structural_element: Lattice Energies from the Born-Haber Cycle

explanations_tiered.definition: The Born-Haber cycle is a thermochemical cycle applying Hess's law to calculate lattice energy from experimentally measurable quantities.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Lattice energy cannot be measured directly but is calculated via the Born-Haber cycle using formation enthalpy and other step enthalpies.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of climbing a mountain then going down: the total height change (lattice energy) is found by adding all the ups and downs along a different path.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Hess's law states the total enthalpy change is independent of path. The direct formation from elements is set equal to the sum of sublimation, bond dissociation, IE, EA, and lattice energy. Solving gives \(U = -786 \mathrm{kJ/mol}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The Born-Haber cycle is a closed loop: \(\Delta H_f(\mathrm{NaCl}) = \Delta H_{\text{sub}}(\mathrm{Na}) + \frac{1}{2}D(\mathrm{Cl_2}) + \mathrm{IE}_1(\mathrm{Na}) + \mathrm{EA}(\mathrm{Cl}) + U(\mathrm{NaCl})\). Solving for \(U\) yields \(-786 \mathrm{kJ/mol}\), confirming the enormous stability of the ionic lattice.
worked_solution: Step 2: Sum the energy inputs: \(108 + 120 + 496 = 724\) then add EA: \(724 + (-349) = 375\ \mathrm{kJ/mol}\).
reasoning_steps: Write the Born-Haber cycle equation.
Substitute given values into the equation.
Perform arithmetic carefully with signs.
Select the matching calculated lattice energy.
misconceptions: Lattice energy is always positive (endothermic). False: lattice energy is defined as energy released, so it is negative in the Born-Haber equation as written here.

glossary_entry.definition: Application of Hess's law to calculate lattice energy of ionic compounds.

flashcard.front: How is lattice energy calculated using the Born-Haber cycle?
flashcard.back: By summing known enthalpy changes (sublimation, bond dissociation, IE, EA) and subtracting from the formation enthalpy.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Calculate lattice energy given \(\Delta H_f = -411\), sublimation = 108, \(\frac{1}{2}D = 120\), IE = 496, EA = -349 (kJ/mol).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Method to calculate lattice energy using Hess's law.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize sign conventions: lattice energy is negative in this formulation.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate lattice energy from given data

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subsection: Factors Affecting the Formation of Ionic Bonds
questions.structural_anchor: Factors Affecting the Formation of Ionic Bonds
questions.question: How do ionization energy (IE) and electron affinity (EA) affect the likelihood of ionic bond formation between a metal M and a nonmetal X?

options.A: Low IE of M and low EA of X favour ionic bonding.
options.B: High IE of M and high EA of X favour ionic bonding.
options.C: Low IE of M and high EA of X favour ionic bonding.
options.D: High IE of M and low EA of X favour ionic bonding.

questions.hint: The metal must easily lose electrons (low IE) and the nonmetal must readily gain them (high EA).
questions.explanation: Ionic bond formation requires electron transfer from metal to nonmetal. A low ionization energy makes it easy for the metal to form a cation. A high (more negative) electron affinity makes the nonmetal readily accept an electron to form an anion.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Factors Affecting the Formation of Ionic Bonds
source_trace.structural_element: Factors Affecting the Formation of Ionic Bonds
source_trace.content_anchor: low ionization energy of a metal atom and high electron affinity of a non-metal atom facilitate the formation of an ionic bond

explanations_tiered.definition: Ionization energy is the energy required to remove an electron from a gaseous atom; electron affinity is the energy change when an atom gains an electron.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Low IE means the metal easily loses electrons; high (negative) EA means the nonmetal strongly attracts electrons. Both favor electron transfer and ionic bond formation.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If a metal doesn't hold its electrons tightly (low IE) and a nonmetal really wants electrons (high EA), they will transfer electrons easily and form an ionic bond.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: A low ionization energy (e.g., Na: 496 kJ/mol) facilitates cation formation. A high (more negative) electron affinity (e.g., Cl: -349 kJ/mol) favors anion formation. Together they promote electron transfer.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The thermodynamic driving force for ionic bonding is the net energy change. Low IE reduces the cost of cation formation; high EA increases the energy released upon anion formation. Combined, they lower the overall endothermicity before lattice energy release.
reasoning_steps: Recall IE: energy to remove e⁻ from metal (low IE = easy loss).
Recall EA: energy change when nonmetal gains e⁻ (more negative = more favorable).
Combine: low IE + high (negative) EA → favorable electron transfer.
misconceptions: High ionization energy favores ionic bonding. False; high IE makes it harder to remove electrons, opposing bond formation.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Ionic bond favored by: low IE (metal) + high EA (nonmetal).

glossary_entry.definition: Energy released when an atom in the gas phase gains an electron.

flashcard.front: What combination of IE and EA favors ionic bonding?
flashcard.back: Low IE of metal and high (negative) EA of nonmetal.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why do alkali metals and halogens readily form ionic bonds?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Alkali metals have very low IE; halogens have very high (negative) EA.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Energy required to remove an electron from a gaseous atom.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast IE/EA trends on the periodic table.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Relate IE and EA to ionic bond formation
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Low IE facilitates electron loss; high EA facilitates electron gain, promoting electron transfer.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subsection: Factors Affecting the Formation of Ionic Bonds
questions.structural_anchor: Factors Affecting the Formation of Ionic Bonds
questions.question: The lattice energy (\(U\)) of an ionic compound is proportional to \(\frac{q_1 q_2}{r}\). Which statement correctly describes how ionic charges and radii affect \(U\)?

options.A: U increases with larger ionic radii and smaller charges.
options.B: U decreases with smaller ionic radii and larger charges.
options.C: U increases with larger ionic charges and smaller radii.
options.D: U is independent of charge and radius.

questions.hint: Coulomb's law: force is stronger when charges are large and distance is small.
questions.explanation: Lattice energy is directly proportional to the product of the ionic charges (\(q_1 \times q_2\)) and inversely proportional to the distance between ions (\(r\)). Therefore, larger charges and smaller radii lead to a larger (more negative) lattice energy.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Factors Affecting the Formation of Ionic Bonds
source_trace.structural_element: Factors Affecting the Formation of Ionic Bonds

explanations_tiered.definition: Lattice energy is the energy released when gaseous ions combine to form one mole of an ionic solid.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Coulomb's law governs lattice energy: greater charge and shorter distance increase electrostatic attraction, making U more negative (stronger bond).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of magnets: stronger magnets (bigger charges) and holding them closer (smaller radius) makes them stick together harder — that's higher lattice energy.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From \(U \propto \frac{q_1 q_2}{r}\), if \(q_1\) and \(q_2\) increase, the product increases, raising \(U\). If \(r\) decreases, the denominator shrinks, also raising \(U\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The proportionality comes from Coulomb's law for electrostatic potential energy: \(E = k \frac{q_1 q_2}{r}\). Higher charges increase attractive energy; smaller ionic radii (due to greater nuclear pull) decrease distance, magnifying the energy release upon lattice formation.
reasoning_steps: Recall the formula: \(U \propto (q_1 \times q_2) / r\).
Larger numerator (q1 × q2) increases U.
Smaller denominator (r) increases U.
Thus larger charges and smaller radii increase U.
misconceptions: Larger ions increase lattice energy. False: larger r decreases U.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Lattice energy ∝ (charge1 × charge2) / distance. Higher charge, smaller distance → stronger.

glossary_entry.definition: Energy released when ions form an ionic solid from gaseous state.

flashcard.front: How do ionic charge and radius affect lattice energy?
flashcard.back: Larger charges and smaller radii increase lattice energy (more negative).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which has greater lattice energy: MgO or NaCl? Why?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: MgO: Mg²⁺ and O²⁻ have larger charges than Na⁺ and Cl⁻, and smaller radii, so higher U.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Energy change when gaseous ions form solid ionic lattice.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare MgO vs NaCl to illustrate charge effect.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Predict relative lattice energies from ion charges and sizes
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. U ∝ (q₁q₂)/r, so larger charges and smaller radii increase U.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subsection: 2.2.3 Exceptions to the Octet Rule in Ionic Compounds
questions.structural_anchor: Less than Octet (Central Atom is Deficient of Electrons)
questions.question: A student reasons that because beryllium is in Group 2, its ion should have the electron configuration of neon. However, the \(\mathrm{Be^{2+}}\) ion actually has which electron configuration and why is it stable?

options.A: \(1s^2\), stable like helium; Be does not need an octet.
options.B: \(1s^2 2s^2 2p^6\), stable like neon; Be follows octet rule.
options.C: \(1s^2 2s^2\), stable like helium; Be has only 2 valence electrons.
options.D: \(1s^2 2s^2 2p^2\), stable like carbon; Be shares electrons.

questions.hint: Beryllium is in Period 2 and loses both valence electrons to form \(\mathrm{Be^{2+}}\).
questions.explanation: Beryllium has the electron configuration \(1s^2 2s^2\). It loses the two 2s electrons to form \(\mathrm{Be^{2+}}\), which has the configuration \(1s^2\), isoelectronic with helium. Beryllium is an exception to the octet rule, achieving stability with only 2 electrons.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 2.2.3 Exceptions to the Octet Rule in Ionic Compounds
source_trace.structural_element: Less than Octet (Central Atom is Deficient of Electrons)
source_trace.content_anchor: beryllium ion (Be^{2+}) is isoelectronic with He

explanations_tiered.definition: An exception to the octet rule occurs when an atom in a compound has fewer than eight valence electrons (electron-deficient).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Beryllium and boron often form stable compounds with only 4 or 6 valence electrons, respectively, achieving a duet (like He) or sextet rather than an octet.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine a tiny atom like beryllium. It's so small that it's happy with just 2 electrons in its outer shell, like helium. It doesn't need 8.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Be has 4 electrons total: \(1s^2 2s^2\). When it loses both 2s electrons to become \(\mathrm{Be^{2+}}\), its configuration is \(1s^2\), which is the stable helium configuration. An octet is not necessary.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The octet rule is a useful guideline but fails for very small atoms. \(\mathrm{Be^{2+}}\) has no 2p orbitals occupied; its stability comes from the filled 1s shell (duet rule). The high charge density of \(\mathrm{Be^{2+}}\) leads to strong lattice energy in compounds like BeO.
reasoning_steps: Write ground-state electron configuration of Be: \(1s^2 2s^2\).
Remove two electrons to form \(\mathrm{Be^{2+}}\).
Resulting configuration: \(1s^2\).
Recognize this is isoelectronic with He (2 electrons), not Ne.
misconceptions: All ions must have noble gas configurations with 8 valence electrons. False; small ions can have 2 (He configuration).

glossary_entry.definition: Tendency of hydrogen, helium, lithium, and beryllium to achieve a stable two-electron configuration.

flashcard.front: Why is Be²⁺ stable despite having only 2 electrons?
flashcard.back: It has the stable helium configuration (1s²), following the duet rule instead of the octet rule.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the electron configuration of Be²⁺ and why is it stable?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: 1s², stable because it is isoelectronic with helium (duet rule).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Stability of a two-electron configuration like helium.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Point out that Be and B are electron-deficient in many compounds.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Identify exceptions to the octet rule in ions
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Be²⁺ has 1s² (He configuration), stable via duet rule, not octet.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subsection: 2.2.3 Exceptions to the Octet Rule in Ionic Compounds
questions.question: Why do transition metal ions such as \(\mathrm{Zn^{2+}}\) and \(\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}\) often have more than eight electrons in their valence shells, in contrast to main group ions?

options.A: Transition metals cannot lose electrons and keep them in d orbitals.
options.B: The d orbitals can participate in bonding, allowing up to 18 valence electrons.
options.C: Transition metals always lose all their d electrons first.
options.D: The octet rule applies only to nonmetals, not to metals.

questions.hint: Consider the electron configuration and the availability of d orbitals.
questions.explanation: Transition metals have d orbitals in their valence shell. When they form ions, electrons are lost from the s orbital first, but d electrons remain. The total valence electrons (including d) can be up to 18, known as the 18-electron rule. For example, \(\mathrm{Zn^{2+}}\) has \(3d^{10}\), giving 18 valence electrons (3s²3p⁶3d¹⁰).

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 2.2.3 Exceptions to the Octet Rule in Ionic Compounds

explanations_tiered.definition: The 18-electron rule states that transition metal compounds are stable when the metal has 18 valence electrons (filled s, p, and d subshells).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Unlike main group elements that follow the octet (8 e⁻), transition metals can use their d orbitals, achieving 18 electrons (ns²np⁶nd¹⁰). Ions like Zn²⁺ keep the filled 3d¹⁰ subshell.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Transition metals have extra hiding spots for electrons called d orbitals. They can hold up to 10 more electrons than main group elements, so they often have 18 total in their outer energy levels.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Electron configuration of Zn: [Ar]4s²3d¹⁰. Zn²⁺ loses the 4s², leaving [Ar]3d¹⁰. The valence shell includes 3s, 3p, and 3d orbitals, holding 2+6+10 = 18 electrons, not just 8.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The stability of the 18-electron configuration arises from the complete filling of the (n-1)d, ns, and np subshells. This is common in low-spin octahedral complexes and explains the existence of ions like \(\mathrm{Zn^{2+}}\) and \(\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}\) (which has 3d⁵, not 18 but still exceeds octet).
reasoning_steps: Recall that transition metals have d orbitals in the valence shell.
Count valence electrons possible: s² (2) + p⁶ (6) + d¹⁰ (10) = 18.
Recognize that ions may retain d electrons even after losing s electrons.
Thus they commonly exceed the octet.
misconceptions: Transition metal ions always lose all d electrons. False; they lose s electrons first, often keeping d electrons.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Transition metals: valence = ns² + (n-1)d, total up to 18 e⁻.

glossary_entry.definition: Stability rule for transition metal compounds where the metal has 18 valence electrons.

flashcard.front: Why do transition metal ions like Zn²⁺ not obey the octet rule?
flashcard.back: They have accessible d orbitals, allowing up to 18 valence electrons (e.g., Zn²⁺ has 3d¹⁰ in its valence shell).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Stability rule for transition metals where 18 valence electrons (filled s, p, d) are optimal.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Principle that transition metals achieve stability with 18 valence electrons.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Transition metals: can have 18 e⁻ (d orbitals).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with main group octet rule; introduce d-orbital involvement.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain exceptions to octet rule for transition metals
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. d orbitals allow valence electron count up to 18, exceeding the octet.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subsection: 2.2.3 Exceptions to the Octet Rule in Ionic Compounds
questions.question: Which set of ions all have electron configurations that exceed the octet (i.e., have more than 8 valence electrons)?
questions.hint: Transition metals often retain d electrons after ionization.
questions.explanation: \(\mathrm{Zn^{2+}}\) has \(3d^{10}\), \(\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}\) has \(3d^{5}\), and \(\mathrm{Cu^+}\) has \(3d^{10}\). All have electrons in d subshells, giving total valence electrons (3s²3p⁶3dⁿ) greater than 8. The other options involve main group ions with noble gas configurations (8 valence electrons).

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 2.2.3 Exceptions to the Octet Rule in Ionic Compounds

explanations_tiered.definition: The 18-electron rule refers to stability when transition metal ions have 18 valence electrons (including d electrons), exceeding the octet.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Main group ions (Groups 1,2,13-18) typically achieve noble gas configurations (8 valence electrons). Transition metal ions retain d electrons, often giving 9–18 valence electrons.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Some ions are like suitcases that can hold 8 clothes (octet). Transition metal ions are like bigger suitcases that can hold up to 18 clothes because they have extra pockets (d orbitals).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(\mathrm{Zn^{2+}}\) configuration: [Ar]3d¹⁰. The valence shell (n=3) includes 3s²3p⁶3d¹⁰ = 18 electrons. \(\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}\): [Ar]3d⁵ gives 13 valence electrons. Both exceed 8.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The (n-1)d subshell is part of the valence shell for transition metals. Even after losing ns electrons, the (n-1)d electrons remain, giving total valence counts from 9 to 18. This explains the expanded electron count.
reasoning_steps: Recall that main group ions achieve octet (ns²np⁶).
Recall that transition metals have partially filled d orbitals in their ions.
Identify which option contains transition metal ions.
Confirm those ions retain d electrons → exceed octet.
misconceptions: All ions have noble gas configurations. False; transition metal ions often do not.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Transition metal ions: retain d e⁻ → >8 valence e⁻.

glossary_entry.definition: The outermost electron shell of an atom, which contains the valence electrons involved in bonding.
glossary_entry.example_equation: For Zn, n=4 shell contains 4s², but for Zn²⁺, n=3 shell (3s²3p⁶3d¹⁰) is considered valence.

flashcard.front: Which type of ions commonly have more than 8 valence electrons?
flashcard.back: Transition metal ions, due to retained d electrons (e.g., Zn²⁺, Fe³⁺).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Give an example of an ion with more than 8 valence electrons.
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Zn²⁺ has 3d¹⁰ in its valence shell for a total of 18 valence electrons.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The highest principal energy level containing electrons.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Transition metal ions: often >8 valence e⁻.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce that d electrons are counted in the valence shell for transition metals.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Classify ions by octet rule adherence
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Zn²⁺, Fe³⁺, Cu⁺ are transition metal ions with d electrons, exceeding 8 valence electrons.
